---
title: "Furrballs: A NUMA-Aware, High-Performance Data Platform for Low-Latency Server Workloads"
subtitle: "White Paper Draft"
author: "NuAtlas"
date: last-modified
lang: en
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: true
    documentclass: scrartcl
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry:
      - top=1in
      - bottom=1in
      - left=1in
      - right=1in
    linestretch: 1.12
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{microtype}
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{caption}
        \captionsetup{font=small,labelfont=bf}
        \usepackage[automark]{scrlayer-scrpage}
        \clearpairofpagestyles
        \ihead{Furrballs White Paper}
        \ohead{\pagemark}
        \KOMAoptions{headsepline=true}
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---

## Status
Draft v0.3 (living document)

## Abstract
Furrballs is a high-performance, NUMA-aware caching library designed for latency-sensitive workloads where memory locality, cache policy behavior, and tail-latency predictability are critical. The core thesis contribution is using NUMA topology as a first-class input to cache placement and eviction decisions --- an approach not found in existing systems. The library combines per-node page allocation with ARC (Adaptive Replacement Cache) eviction, backed by RocksDB persistence. Development follows a layered approach: NUMA-aware page caching as the primary contribution, with tiered intra-page optimization and adaptive memory pooling (AMP) as future extensions.

This white paper documents the problem space, design decisions and their rationale, architecture, and measurement plan. It also serves as a record of design discussions and tradeoffs made during development.

## Project Narrative and Rescoping
Furrballs evolved through several scope changes that matter for both technical context and decision traceability:

1. Initial motivation: investigate NUMA-aware caching after observing that popular systems such as Redis are not strongly NUMA-aware for all deployment scenarios.
2. Practical inspiration: build a cache-first library influenced by Ristretto's simplicity and performance-oriented design goals.
3. Constraint phase: no direct access to NUMA hardware, so the idea expanded into simulating distribution pressure by spreading cache behavior across multiple machines. This led to scope creep: virtual pointers, AMP, Burst mode, LockablePage, slab allocation, and S3FIFO/LRU/LFU policy variants were all designed during this phase.
4. Downscope phase: temporary shift toward game-engine use cases (the author's strongest domain) to ensure continuous progress and ship a usable core.
5. Current rescope (April 2026): return to the NUMA-aware objective as the primary focus. All scope-creep features were stripped. The project now has a clear, defensible thesis contribution: NUMA-aware page placement as a cache policy input.

This narrative is intentional, not accidental. Each scope change reduced risk, increased implementation confidence, or unlocked a clearer path to validation.

## 1. Problem Statement
Modern low-latency services often degrade under memory pressure, irregular access patterns, and multi-socket contention. Typical bottlenecks include:

- Tail-latency spikes from cache misses and allocator fragmentation.
- Performance loss from non-local NUMA memory access (remote access can be 1.2-2x slower than local).
- Operational complexity in combining cache, compression, and persistence layers.
- Difficulty validating behavior consistently across different hardware and deployment models.

Furrballs targets these bottlenecks with an architecture that prioritizes locality, predictable behavior, and modular policy design.

### 1.1 The NUMA Gap in Existing Systems
NUMA-aware allocation exists in general-purpose allocators (TCMalloc, jemalloc) but they don't address cache eviction or page-level data management. Meta's CacheLib adds NUMA sharding (separate cache instances per node) but this is static, coarse-grained partitioning --- not adaptive per-page placement based on accessing thread locality. No published work combines NUMA topology as an input to adaptive cache eviction policy. This is Furrballs' contribution.

## 2. Goals and Non-Goals

### Goals
- Low and stable latency under mixed read/write load.
- NUMA-aware allocation and access behavior as the primary thesis contribution.
- Modular cache policy layer with ARC as the initial eviction strategy.
- Progressive delivery through phased architecture evolution.
- Reproducible performance evaluation and transparent tradeoff reporting.

### Non-Goals (current phase)
- General-purpose database replacement.
- Broad feature parity with large data platforms.
- Platform-specific tuning for all operating systems at once.
- Protocol design or client/server architecture (deferred to a later phase).
- In-page memory management (bump allocator, value reordering --- deferred to Layer 2).

## 3. Design Principles
- Locality first: optimize for CPU and memory locality before adding features.
- Explicit tradeoffs: prefer clear, measurable behavior over opaque heuristics.
- Incremental validation: every subsystem change should map to benchmark deltas.
- Modularity: cache policies, memory strategies, and transport should evolve independently.
- Strip then build: remove scope creep before adding new features. Every feature must serve the NUMA thesis contribution.

## 4. Design Decisions Log

This section records significant design decisions made during development, along with their rationale and any alternatives considered.

### 4.1 Per-Page Allocation Instead of Slab Allocation
**Decision:** Allocate pages individually via the memory manager (or NUMA-aware allocator), not as a contiguous slab.

**Rationale:** Slab allocation forces all pages onto one NUMA node (contiguous memory cannot span nodes). Per-page allocation allows each page to be placed on the NUMA node of the thread that requests it. This is the mechanism that enables NUMA-aware placement. The tradeoff is loss of spatial locality guarantees between pages and slightly higher per-page allocation overhead, but this overhead is paid once per cold miss, not per request.

**Rejected alternative:** Slab allocation with virtual pointer indirection. This was the original design but introduced complexity (address space reduction, debugging difficulty, NUMA conflicts) without serving the thesis contribution.

### 4.2 ARC as the Sole Cache Policy (Initially)
**Decision:** Implement ARC (Adaptive Replacement Cache) as the only eviction policy. S3FIFO, LRU, and LFU are deferred.

**Rationale:** ARC adaptively balances between recency and frequency, which is sufficient to demonstrate the NUMA placement effect. Implementing multiple policies before having benchmark data would be premature optimization. Additional policies can be added as future work or comparison baselines.

### 4.3 Write-Back with Dirty Tracking
**Decision:** Use write-back caching. `Set()` writes to the in-memory page and marks it dirty. Dirty pages are flushed to RocksDB only on eviction or explicit shutdown.

**Rationale:** Write-back minimizes disk I/O for hot data. The alternative (write-through) would make every `Set()` pay RocksDB latency, making it impossible to isolate the NUMA placement variable in benchmarks. The `IsVolatile` flag in `FurrConfig` allows skipping persistence entirely for game-engine use cases.

### 4.4 RocksDB Block Cache Disabled
**Decision:** Set RocksDB's block cache to size 0. Furrballs owns all caching responsibility.

**Rationale:** Having two cache layers (Furrballs' ARC + RocksDB's internal LRU) means caching cached data, adding unnecessary lookup overhead and making it impossible to attribute latency improvements to the NUMA placement. By disabling RocksDB's cache, the only cache behavior is Furrballs' own.

### 4.5 Key-Based API (In Progress)
**Decision:** The public API uses string keys: `Set(string key, void* data, size_t size)` and `Get(string key)`. The old virtual-address-based API is retained internally but deprecated for user-facing code.

**Rationale:** Virtual addresses are an implementation detail. Users think in terms of keys. The key-to-page mapping is handled internally by the `KeyStore` (an in-memory `unordered_map<string, KeyMeta>`) and persisted to RocksDB for recovery.

### 4.6 Multi-Value Pages with Bump Allocator (Planned)
**Decision:** A single page can hold multiple key-value pairs. Values are packed sequentially via a bump allocator (page header tracks `used_bytes`, new values append from the start). `KeyMeta` stores `{PageIndex, DataSize, DataOffset}` for each key.

**Rationale:** One key per page wastes memory (a 64-byte value in a 4KB page = 98.4% waste). Multi-value pages amortize allocation overhead and exploit spatial locality. The bump allocator is chosen for simplicity --- write-once/read-many workloads don't need in-page free lists or compaction initially.

**Deferred to Layer 2:** Value reordering within pages (frequency-based clustering), cold value migration between pages, and in-page compaction.

### 4.7 Internal Thread Pool (NodeJob)
**Decision:** Furrballs spawns and manages its own worker threads internally via the `NodeJob` class. One worker per NUMA node, pinned via `Numatic::PinCurrentThreadToNode()` at thread start. Users do not interact with `NodeJob` directly.

**Rationale:** External `numactl` pinning is a benchmark tool, not a deployment strategy. The library should handle NUMA pinning internally so users get correct behavior by default. The `NodeJob` class is non-copyable, non-movable, and its lifetime is bounded by `Bootstrap()` to `Shutdown()`.

### 4.8 Numatic: Platform Abstraction Layer
**Decision:** NUMA operations are abstracted behind `Numatic`, a separate, stateless, functional-style library within the project. Furrballs calls `Numatic::PinCurrentThreadToNode()`, `Numatic::AllocateOnNode()`, etc. --- no `#ifdef _WIN32` or platform-specific code in Furrballs itself.

**Rationale:** Platform-specific code (libnuma on Linux, Windows NUMA APIs) is isolated into `NumaticUnix.cpp` and `NumaticWin.cpp`, selected by CMake. This keeps Furrballs' code platform-agnostic. Numatic uses free functions (no classes) because NUMA operations are stateless system-level queries with no object to encapsulate. A future extraction to a standalone library is planned.

### 4.9 Logical vs Physical Page Size
**Decision:** `FurrConfig::PageSize` is purely logical --- it defines how data is divided and how keys map to pages. The physical allocation size can differ: when `NumaConfig::AllocateUsingNodePageSize = true`, the allocator uses `numa_pagesize()` (2MB with huge pages) instead of `FurrConfig::PageSize`. Multiple logical pages fit inside one physical allocation.

**Rationale:** Separating logical and physical page size means `PageSize` controls data organization while NUMA controls memory granularity. This gives the best of both: fine-grained key mapping and large, TLB-efficient physical allocations on servers.

### 4.10 NumaConfig as Stack-Allocated Pointer (Vulkan-Style)
**Decision:** `FurrConfig` holds `NumaConfig* numaConfig`, where the user passes a pointer to their stack-allocated `NumaConfig`. Furrballs copies the values it needs during `CreateBall()` and never stores the pointer.

**Rationale:** `NumaConfig` is read-once at creation time. No heap allocation needed, no lifetime concerns. The pattern mirrors Vulkan's `VkInstanceCreateInfo` approach.

### 4.11 Shared Mutex for Read/Write Concurrency
**Decision:** `FurrBall` uses `std::shared_mutex`. `Get()` takes a shared lock (multiple readers). `Set()` and miss-path allocation take exclusive locks.

**Rationale:** In the current blocking design, shared reads allow concurrent cache lookups without contention. The lock is per-FurrBall instance, not per-page, to avoid the overhead of thousands of mutexes at 4KB granularity.

### 4.12 Error Codes Over Exceptions
**Decision:** The new key-based API returns `Error` enum values (e.g., `NO_ERR`, `INVALID_ARG`, `OUT_OF_MEM`). No exceptions in the public API.

**Rationale:** Consistent with game-engine and systems programming conventions. The `Error` enum is organized by category (general, initialization, memory, cache, storage, compression, threading, I/O) for easy filtering.

## 5. High-Level Architecture

### 5.1 Layered Cache Model
Furrballs implements a tiered cache hierarchy:

- **L1 (Hot, in-memory):** ARC-managed pages with NUMA-aware allocation. Values are packed into pages via bump allocator. This is the primary thesis contribution.
- **L2 (Cold, persisted):** RocksDB backing store. Fast enough to not dominate benchmark overhead, provides durability and the cold-read path.

L1 is the NUMA-aware layer. L2 is the persistence layer. The benchmark comparison isolates the NUMA variable: same ARC, same RocksDB, same workload --- only the allocation policy differs.

### 5.2 Future Layers (Documented, Not Implemented)
- **L1 value demotion:** Instead of evicting an entire page, demote low-priority values within the page first. Requires value-level frequency tracking in `KeyMeta`.
- **L2 page eviction:** Flush entire pages to RocksDB when the page is mostly cold.
- **L3 adaptive memory pooling (AMP):** Dynamically grow/shrink the page pool based on eviction pressure. Track eviction rate, expand pool when pressure is high, contract when idle.

Each layer is independently measurable, which is required for the thesis structure.

### 5.3 Core Components
- **Memory layer:** Per-page allocation via `MemoryManager` with thread-local tracking and NUMA-aware placement through Numatic.
- **Cache layer:** ARC policy with write-back dirty tracking. Key-to-page mapping via `KeyStore`.
- **Storage layer:** RocksDB with block cache disabled. Furrballs owns all caching.
- **Threading layer:** `NodeJob` per NUMA node with condition-variable-based job dispatch. Workers pinned at startup.

### 5.4 Data Path (Key-Based)
1. User calls `Set(key, data, size)` or `Get(key)`.
2. `KeyStore` maps the key to `KeyMeta {PageIndex, DataSize, DataOffset}`.
3. If page is in ARC cache (hit), return data at offset. NUMA-local access.
4. If page is not in cache (miss), allocate a new page on the current thread's NUMA node, load from RocksDB if persisted data exists, insert into ARC.
5. On eviction, dirty pages are flushed to RocksDB. Key metadata is persisted.

## 6. NUMA-Aware Design

### 6.1 Approach
The core hypothesis: per-page NUMA-aware placement, where each page is allocated on the NUMA node of the thread that first accesses it, produces measurable p99 improvement over naive allocation.

### 6.2 Mechanism
- `Bootstrap()` detects topology via `Numatic::GetNodeCount()`.
- One `NodeJob` worker is spawned per NUMA node, pinned to that node.
- `AllocatePage()` uses `Numatic::AllocateOnNode()` with the current thread's node.
- `AllocateMemoryNUMA()` allocates on the calling thread's node via `numa_alloc_onnode()`.
- All subsequent accesses to that page from the same node are local (distance 10). Cross-node accesses incur distance 20 (2x latency in QEMU; 1.2-1.5x on real hardware).

### 6.3 Benchmark Isolation
The NUMA variable is isolated by comparing:
- **NUMA-aware**: Pages allocated on the owning node via `AllocateMemoryNUMA()`.
- **Naive baseline**: Pages allocated via regular `AllocateMemory()` (no NUMA preference, OS decides placement).

Same ARC, same RocksDB, same workload, same thread count. Only the allocation policy differs.

### 6.4 NUMA Simulation
Development uses QEMU with a 2-node topology:
```
Node 0: CPUs 0-2, 2GB RAM
Node 1: CPUs 3-5, 2GB RAM
Node distances: local=10, remote=20
```
This provides correct NUMA semantics for code-path validation. Real hardware benchmarks are needed for publishable performance numbers.

### 6.5 Related Work Comparison
| System | NUMA Approach | Difference from Furrballs |
|---|---|---|
| TCMalloc | NUMA-aware allocation | Allocator only; no cache eviction or page management |
| jemalloc | NUMA-aware arenas | Allocator only; no cache policy |
| CacheLib (Meta) | Static sharding per node | Coarse-grained; entire cache instance per node, not adaptive per-page |
| Redis | No NUMA awareness | Baseline comparison point |
| Furrballs | Per-page placement + ARC | Fine-grained, adaptive, page-level NUMA locality as policy input |

## 7. Server/Client Expansion Plan (Deferred)
Protocol, server, and client layers are deferred to a later phase. The current focus is proving the NUMA thesis with the library core. When implemented:
- Server wraps Core and hosts cache state.
- Client communicates via a minimal binary protocol (fixed-size opcodes: GET, SET, DELETE, etc.).
- Protocol is a thin transport layer, not a research contribution.

## 8. Evaluation Methodology

### 8.1 Metrics
Primary metrics:
- Read latency: p50, p95, p99.
- Write latency: p50, p95, p99.
- Throughput: operations per second.
- Cache hit ratio.
- Memory overhead and fragmentation behavior.
- NUMA locality indicators (local vs remote memory access ratio).

### 8.2 Workload Profiles
- Read-heavy steady-state.
- Write-heavy burst.
- Mixed workload with skewed key popularity.
- Multi-threaded access with threads pinned to different NUMA nodes.

### 8.3 Benchmark Matrix
- NUMA-aware allocation vs naive allocation (primary comparison).
- Single-node vs multi-node access patterns.
- Cache capacity pressure (varying ARC capacity).
- Thread count scaling sweep.

### 8.4 Reproducibility Requirements
- Fixed build configuration per benchmark run.
- Logged hardware and kernel/runtime metadata.
- Versioned benchmark scripts and datasets.
- Raw results retained with timestamp and commit identifier.

## 9. Claims and Evidence Policy
Any performance claim in this white paper should include:
- The exact workload profile.
- The hardware/software test environment.
- Comparison baseline and configuration parity.
- Statistical summary from repeated runs.

## 10. Current Status
Core library compiles and runs with:
- ARC cache policy with eviction callback and dirty page flushing.
- Per-page allocation with NUMA-aware path.
- RocksDB persistence with block cache disabled.
- Numatic platform abstraction layer (Linux implementation complete).
- NodeJob per-node worker threading (in progress).
- Key-based Set/Get API (stub, awaiting bump allocator).

The project is on the `numa-focus` branch. Development and testing use a QEMU NUMA VM (2 nodes, 6 vCPUs, 4GB RAM).

## 11. Risks and Open Questions
- Which NUMA strategies provide best p99 gains for the target workload mix?
- What is the throughput vs tail-latency tradeoff at high concurrency?
- Can QEMU NUMA simulation produce meaningful latency differentials, or is real hardware required for publishable results?
- How should cache policy adapt under rapidly changing access distributions?
- Is per-page NUMA placement sufficient, or is request routing (forwarding operations to the owning node's worker) necessary for additional gains?

## 12. Milestone-Driven Roadmap

### Phase 1: NUMA-Aware Core (Current)
- Per-page NUMA-aware allocation and caching.
- Key-based Set/Get with bump allocator.
- Worker thread spawning and pinning per NUMA node.
- Benchmark harness comparing NUMA-aware vs naive allocation.
- Target: reproducible p99 numbers on QEMU NUMA VM.

### Phase 2: Per-Page Optimization
- Value reordering within pages (frequency-based clustering).
- Cold value migration between pages.
- In-page compaction.
- Comparison: ordered vs unordered pages, same workload.

### Phase 3: Adaptive Memory Pooling (AMP)
- Dynamic page pool growth/contraction under eviction pressure.
- Pages as cache tiers (L1 hot, L2 warm, evicted = RocksDB).
- Comparison: static pool vs AMP, same workload.

### Phase 4: Server + Client + Protocol
- Furrballs Server wrapping Core.
- Furrballs Client with minimal binary protocol.
- Cross-process NUMA benchmarks.

### Phase 5: Publication
- Formalize benchmark results.
- Write thesis/paper with reproducible evidence.

## 13. Conclusion
Furrballs aims to demonstrate that explicit NUMA-aware page placement in a cache system produces measurable p99 improvement over naive allocation. The contribution is not a new allocator (TCMalloc, jemalloc exist) or a new cache policy (ARC is well-studied) --- it is the combination of NUMA topology as a first-class input to cache placement and eviction decisions at the page level. No published work addresses this specific intersection. The tiered architecture (per-value, per-page, pool-level) provides multiple independently measurable optimization layers, supporting a structured thesis narrative.

## Appendix A: Results Table Template
| Scenario | Threads | NUMA Policy | Read p99 (us) | Write p99 (us) | Throughput (ops/s) | Hit Ratio | Local/Remote Ratio | Notes |
|---|---:|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Baseline (naive alloc) |  | None |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| NUMA-aware |  | Per-page |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| NUMA-aware + huge pages |  | Per-page + node page size |  |  |  |  |  |  |

## Appendix B: Environment Template
- CPU model and socket count:
- NUMA node topology:
- RAM size and speed:
- Kernel and OS version:
- Compiler and flags:
- Build type:
- Dataset profile:
- Commit identifier:

## Appendix C: Repository Structure
```
Furrballs/
  Furrballs/          # Core library
    include/
      Furrballs.h     # Public API, ARC policy, configs
      Page.h          # Page data structure
      Logger.h        # Thread-safe logging
      Error.h         # Error code enum
      NodeJob.h       # Per-node worker threading
      IFactory.h      # Factory pattern utility
      MemoryManager.h # Allocation with thread-local tracking
    src/
      Furrballs.cpp   # Core implementation
      Page.cpp        # (Future: bump allocator)
      Logger.cpp      # Platform-safe time formatting
      NodeJob.cpp     # Worker loop and job dispatch
  numatic/            # NUMA platform abstraction
    include/Numatic.h
    src/NumaticUnix.cpp
    src/NumaticWin.cpp
  Sandbox/            # Test executable
  docs/               # White paper and documentation
  vm/                 # QEMU NUMA simulation scripts
```
